title: Introduction to Vector Databases
author: Dr. Sarah Chen
content: Vector databases are specialized storage systems designed to handle high-dimensional vector embeddings. Unlike traditional relational databases that store structured rows and columns, vector databases optimize for similarity search operations. They store data as points in multi-dimensional space, enabling efficient nearest neighbor queries. The core principle behind vector databases is that similar items cluster together in embedding space. When you convert text, images, or other data types into vector embeddings using machine learning models, semantically similar content ends up geographically proximate in the vector space. This property allows vector databases to answer queries like 'find items similar to X' by computing distances between vectors rather than matching exact keywords. Popular vector databases include Pinecone, Weaviate, Qdrant, and Milvus. Many traditional databases now also support vector operations as plugins or built-in features.

title: ANN Algorithms Comparison
author: Prof. James Miller
content: Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) algorithms enable fast similarity search at scale by sacrificing exact accuracy for speed. The most common ANN algorithms include HNSW, IVF, and PQ. HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World) builds a multi-layer graph structure where each layer contains shortcuts through the data. Search begins at the top layer and progressively narrows down. IVF (Inverted File Index) partitions the vector space into clusters and only searches relevant clusters during queries. PQ (Product Quantization) compresses vectors by splitting them into sub-vectors and representing each with a code from a learned codebook. This dramatically reduces memory usage and enables efficient distance computation through lookup tables. HNSW offers excellent query speed with good accuracy but requires significant memory. IVF reduces the search scope but accuracy depends on cluster quality. PQ enables massive scale but may sacrifice some precision. Hybrid approaches combining these methods often achieve the best results.

title: Embedding Models for Text
author: Dr. Maria Rodriguez
content: Embedding models convert text into dense vector representations that capture semantic meaning. Modern embedding models are typically based on transformer architectures like BERT or GPT. The training process learns to position similar words and phrases close together in the embedding space. Popular text embedding models include OpenAI's text-embedding-ada-002, Google's universal sentence encoder, and open-source models like sentence-transformers. The choice of embedding model significantly impacts retrieval quality. Larger models generally produce better embeddings but are slower and more expensive. Domain-specific models trained on specialized data often outperform general models for targeted applications. For example, a model trained on medical literature would better capture relationships in healthcare documents than a general model. Fine-tuning pre-trained models on domain-specific data can further improve performance for particular use cases.

title: RAG System Architecture
author: Dr. Sarah Chen
content: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) combines the power of large language models with external knowledge retrieval. A RAG system consists of three main components: a document processor, a vector store, and an LLM. The document processor ingests source material, chunks it appropriately, and generates embeddings for storage in the vector database. When a user query arrives, the system embeds the query and performs a similarity search to retrieve relevant chunks. These retrieved chunks are then combined with the original query as context for the LLM to generate a response. This architecture allows LLMs to access up-to-date information without fine-tuning and provides a mechanism for source attribution. Key challenges in RAG include choosing the right chunk size, handling multi-modal data, optimizing retrieval precision, and structuring prompts effectively. Advanced RAG techniques include query expansion, reranking, and hybrid search combining keyword and semantic approaches.

title: Source Attribution in AI Systems
author: Prof. Michael Zhang
content: Source attribution in AI systems refers to identifying and tracing the origins of information used in model outputs. For RAG systems specifically, this means connecting generated responses back to the retrieved documents that informed them. Proper attribution serves multiple purposes: it enables fact-checking by allowing users to verify claims, it supports compliance with licensing and copyright requirements, and it builds user trust by promoting transparency. Technical approaches to attribution include storing retrieval logs that record which documents were retrieved for each query, using attention weights to estimate document influence, and implementing citation mechanisms in the output format. The challenge is that LLMs synthesize information from multiple sources, making it difficult to attribute specific claims to specific documents. Some approaches assign confidence scores to each citation based on retrieval relevance, while others use prompting strategies to encourage the model to generate explicit citations. Audit trails that log the entire retrieval and generation pipeline are essential for post-hoc analysis and debugging.

title: Graph Databases for Knowledge Management
author: Dr. Lisa Thompson
content: Graph databases store data as nodes and relationships, making them ideal for representing complex, interconnected information. Unlike relational databases that use tables with foreign keys, graph databases directly model relationships as first-class citizens. This design enables efficient traversal of multi-hop connections that would require expensive JOIN operations in SQL. Neo4j is the most widely used graph database, using the Cypher query language to express graph patterns. Property graphs extend basic graphs by allowing attributes on both nodes and relationships. For knowledge management, graph databases excel at modeling ontologies, hierarchies, and complex relationships between entities. They enable queries like 'find all people who worked on projects with someone who contributed to X' that would be cumbersome in relational databases. The combination of graph databases with vector search enables powerful hybrid queries that can traverse relationships while also searching by semantic similarity.
